content.full_text:
City of Rocklin

3970 Rocklin Road
P.O. Box 1138
Rocklin, CA 95677
(916) 632-4000

January 20, 1992

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95812-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am a member of the City of Rocklin Planning Commission.
This is a request for formal advice concerning "my
participation in City Council review and action on a
proposed subdivision project located near my residence in
the City of Rocklin.

The project, known as The Summit, is proposed for the top of
a hill located to the east of my home within the city of
Rocklin. The developable portion of the project site is a
300 to 800 foot wide hilltop rising 120 feet above the
surrounding neighborhoods. The hill runs essentially
north/south. There is existing residential development
surrounding the hill on three sides: west, south, and east.

The site is currently zoned RE-20. The development request
for a subdivision map and use permit is consistent with the
current zoning. A rezoning to PD-RE-20 is being requested
for technical reasons but does not change the density
allowed on the site. The RE-20 zone is a low density
residential zone, requiring minimum 20,000 square foot lots.

Virtually the entire west side of the hill has a slope
approximating 30 percent, and is not proposed for
development. (The City of Rocklin General Plan contains a
policy prohibiting development on hillsides with a greater
than 20% slope.) This slope is also heavily wooded. Access
to the hilltop, as well as all utility hookups, are planned
for the east side of the hill. The reasons for this design
include the steepness of the slope and the number of trees
on the west side, and the fact that the existing
infrastructure on the east side of the hill was designed to
service the hilltop; a street in an adjacent subdivision
stubs into the logical access to the hilltop, and the
utilities in the subdivisions adjacent to the site on the

Administrative Services Building Department City Hall Community Development
632-4030 632-4030 632-4050 632-4020
FAX 632-4173 FAX 624-4759 FAX 624-8010 FAX 624-4759

Community Services Engineering Department Fire Department Police Department Public Works Department
& Facilities
632-4100 632-4042 632-4150 632-4050 632-4130
FAX 632-4111 FAX 627-4759 FAX 624-2677 FAX 624-2677 FAX 632-4173

Letter to Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 20, 1992
Page 2

east were designed to extend up to the hilltop. As a
result, there will be no development impact from the
development project extending down the western slope.

While not proposed, it is possible that a secondary access
could be designed that would bring traffic down the
northwest side and along Clover Valley Road (see map).
Evidence shows, however, that even if this were done, the
increase in traffic on Clover Valley Road would be nominal.

My home, which I own, is 230 feet from the bottom of the
hillside on the west. The entrance to the project is more
than one mile from my property. From my home, one cannot
see the top of the ridge or any of the developable area.
The distance between my property line and the developable
portion of the property is more than 300 feet. Despite the
proximity of my lot and the project boundary, therefore, my
property is both physically and visually cutoff from the
proposed development.

Attached are maps showing the project site, the location of
my home, and other characteristics of the project.

It is hard to imagine any impact, either positive or
negative, of the development on me or my home. Because of
existing oak trees in the open space area, the slope of the
hill, and the fact that virtually all traffic and services
will access the site from the east side of the hill, I am
unable to see how any decision I may make on the project
could affect my interest in my home.

As a part of the investigation of whether I would be
disqualified from acting on this matter, the City retained a
MAI real estate appraiser to give an opinion on what effect
on value, if any, development on the hilltop would have on
the homes in the surrounding neighborhoods, including the
west side. He concluded it would have no effect whatsoever
on the homes in the neighborhood where I live.

Under California Code of Regulations §18702.3, the effect of
a decision on this project would be deemed material as to my
ownership interest in my home unless the decision would have
absolutely no financial effect on my interest. This rule
applies because my home is within 300 feet of the project
boundary.

Letter to Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
January 20, 1992
Page 3


Based on these facts, my questions are as follows:

1.  What criteria or evidence should a public official use
    to determine whether or not a decision on a development
    project will have a financial effect on that official's
    ownership interest in real property?

2.  Should the slope area located between my home and the
    developable portion of the project site be counted as
    part of the development project for purposes of
    applying §18702.3?

3.  If so, is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision
    on this subdivision project will have a material
    financial effect on my interest in my home,
    distinguishable from its effect on the public
    generally, under Government Code §§87100 and 87103 and
    California Code of Regulations §18702.3?

I understand that the normal turnaround time on requests for
formal advice is 21 working days. This project is scheduled
to begin hearings before the City Planning Commission on
January 23, 1992, with hearings before the City Council to
follow soon thereafter.  I ask that you give this your
attention and render the advice as soon as possible.

If you need any further information in researching and
evaluating this question, or if you have any other questions
of me, you can reach me during the day at 624-4002.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Sherman Moore
Planning Commission Member

Attach.

Box 1138 Rocklin, CA 95677 (916) 632-4000 January 20, 1992 Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95812-0807 Dear Mr. Hallabrin: I am a member of the City of Rocklin Planning Commission. This is a request for formal advice concerning my participation in City Council review and action on a proposed subdivision project located near my residence in the City of Rocklin. The project, known as The Summit, is proposed for the top of a hill located to the east of my home within the City of Rocklin. The developable portion of the project site is a 300 to 800 foot wide hilltop rising 120 feet above the surrounding neighborhoods. The hill runs essentially north/south. There is existing residential development surrounding the hill on three sides: west, south, and east. The site is currently zoned RE-20. The development request for a subdivision map and use permit is consistent with the current zoning. A rezoning to PD-RE-20 is being requested for technical reasons but does not change the density allowed on the site. The RE-20 zone is a low density residential zone, requiring minimum 20,000 square foot lots. Virtually the entire west side of the hill has a slope approximating 30 percent, and is not proposed for development. (The City of Rocklin General Plan contains a policy prohibiting development on hillsides with a greater than 20% slope.) This slope is also heavily wooded. Access to the hilltop, as well as all utility hookups, are planned for the east side of the hill. The reasons for this design include the steepness of the slope and the number of trees on the west side, and the fact that the existing infrastructure on the east side of the hill was designed to service the hilltop: a street in an adjacent subdivision stubs into the logical access to the hilltop, and the utilities in the subdivisions adjacent to the site on the east were designed to extend up to the hilltop. As a result, there will be no development impact from the development project extending down the western slope. While not proposed, it is possible that a secondary access could be designed that would bring traffic down the northwest side and along Clover Valley Road (see map). Evidence shows, however, that even if this were done, the increase in traffic on Clover Valley Road would be nominal. My home, which I own, is 230 feet from the bottom of the hillside on the west. The entrance to the project is more than one mile from my property. From my home, one cannot see the top of the ridge or any of the developable area. The distance between my property line and the developable portion of the project is more than 300 feet. Despite the proximity of my lot and the project boundary, therefore, my property is both physically and visually cutoff from the proposed development. Attached are maps showing the project
